search_results.title: Add "webrtc" CSP directive · Issue #19218 · mdn/content - GitHub
search_results.snippet: Expected to see the "webrtc" directive, which takes a 'block' or 'allow' value. Do you have any supporting links, references, or citations?

search_results.title: Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header - HTTP - MDN Web Docs
search_results.snippet: The HTTP Content-Security-Policy response header allows website administrators to control resources the user agent is allowed to load for a given page.

message.content:
Below is the **CSP Level 3 / MDN directive set that is actually standardized and documented** in the provided sources. I’ve grouped them exactly by the categories you asked for, and I’ve flagged the source/value patterns a security scanner should treat as weaknesses. The W3C CSP Level 3 specification defines the directive framework, and MDN documents the browser-facing behavior and current directive set; both also note that CSP is primarily delivered via the `Content-Security-Policy` response header.[7][3][10]

> **Important scope note:** Your list includes several directives that are *not* all defined by CSP Level 3 as standalone directives in the same way across browsers/spec revisions. I include them below only where the provided W3C/MDN sources support them; for anything outside those sources, I mark the status as *not confirmed by the provided sources* rather than inventing it.[7][3][10]

## Fetch directives

| Directive | Purpose | Insecure values to test for | Scanner should flag | Canonical W3C/MDN URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **default-src** | Sets the fallback source list for resource types not explicitly covered by a more specific fetch directive.[3][5] | `*`, `data:`, `blob:`, `filesystem:`, `'unsafe-inline'`, `'unsafe-eval'`, overly broad host allowlists, missing `'none'` where appropriate.[4][5] | No explicit allowlist for a sensitive app, wildcard fallback, `data:` or `blob:` fallback, use of `'unsafe-inline'`/`'unsafe-eval'` in inherited policies, policy that effectively permits all sources.[4][5] | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **script-src** | Controls where scripts may load from and whether script execution is allowed.[1][3][4] | `'unsafe-inline'`, `'unsafe-eval'`, `data:`, `blob:`, wildcard `*`, broad third-party hosts, missing nonce/hash in strict policies.[4][5] | Inline script allowed without nonce/hash, eval-like sinks allowed, permissive remote script origins, `data:` script sources, missing `object-src 'none'` in XSS-focused policies.[4][5] | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **style-src** | Controls where stylesheets and style execution may come from.[3][5] | `'unsafe-inline'`, `data:`, wildcard `*`, overly broad hosts; style attribute allowance in practice via inline styles.[5] | Inline styles allowed without nonce/hash, permissive remote CSS, `data:` style sources where unnecessary.[5] | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **img-src** | Controls image loading sources.[3][5] | `*`, `data:`, overly broad third-party hosts, `blob:` if unnecessary.[5] | Wildcard image loading, `data:` images where not needed, broad tracking/CDN domains.[5] | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **connect-src** | Controls endpoints for fetch/XHR, WebSocket, EventSource, and similar connections.[3] | `*`, overly broad API domains, `ws:`/`wss:` equivalents when not required, `data:`/`blob:`-style permissiveness where applicable by implementation. | Wildcard exfiltration paths, broad third-party endpoints, mixed HTTP(S) API targets, unnecessary cross-origin telemetry channels.[3] | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **font-src** | Controls font loading sources.[3][5] | `*`, `data:`, broad CDN hosts. | Wildcard font loading, `data:` fonts, unnecessary third-party font hosts. | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **object-src** | Controls plugins/embedded objects such as `object`, `embed`, and `applet`; CSP guidance says to include it or fallback default-src for plugin control.[1][4] | Anything other than `'none'` in modern applications, `*`, broad hosts, `data:`. | `object-src` absent when plugins are possible, `object-src` not set to `'none'`, legacy plugin exposure.[4] | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **media-src** | Controls audio/video/media resource loading.[3][5] | `*`, `data:`, broad third-party hosts. | Wildcard media loading, unnecessary external media/CDN hosts. | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **frame-src** | Controls the sources of nested browsing contexts loaded by the page.[2][3] | `*`, broad iframe hosts, `data:` where accepted by implementation. | Overly broad framing targets, unexpected third-party embedding endpoints.[2][3] | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **child-src** | Legacy/fallback directive historically used for frames and workers; MDN and CSP evolution note it in the fetch-directive family, but it has been superseded by `frame-src` and `worker-src` in modern use.[3] | `*`, broad host lists. | Use in place of more specific modern directives, permissive framing/worker loading, policy ambiguity. | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **worker-src** | Controls sources for workers and service workers.[3] | `*`, broad hosts, `blob:` if not needed. | Worker creation from arbitrary origins, `blob:` worker allowance where unnecessary. | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **manifest-src** | Controls sources for web app manifests.[3] | `*`, broad hosts. | Unrestricted manifest loading, third-party manifest injection paths. | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **prefetch-src** | Controls sources that may be prefetched/prerendered/preconnected by the user agent; MDN documents it as part of CSP’s fetch control set, but support may vary by browser.[3] | `*`, broad hosts. | Unrestricted speculative fetch targets, privacy/data-exfiltration via prefetching. | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |

## Document directives

| Directive | Purpose | Insecure values to test for | Scanner should flag | Canonical W3C/MDN URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **base-uri** | Restricts the URLs that can be used in a document’s `<base>` element.[2][3] | `*`, broad hosts, missing directive entirely. | Missing `base-uri`, allowing attacker-controlled base URL rewriting, broad base allowances.[2][3] | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **sandbox** | Enables a sandboxed environment for the protected resource, similar to the HTML `sandbox` attribute.[7][3] | Missing `sandbox` where isolation is required; overly permissive sandbox tokens when restricting a document. | Absence where a confined execution environment is expected, `allow-scripts`/`allow-same-origin` combinations that undermine isolation, overbroad token sets.[7][3] | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |

## Navigation directives

| Directive | Purpose | Insecure values to test for | Scanner should flag | Canonical W3C/MDN URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **form-action** | Restricts the URLs to which forms may submit.[2][3] | `*`, broad third-party endpoints, `http:` when not required. | Form submission allowed to untrusted origins, phishing/exfiltration endpoints, missing directive.[2][3] | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **frame-ancestors** | Restricts which origins may embed the resource in frames/iframes; MDN and OWASP use it to prevent clickjacking.[2][3] | `*`, broad host lists, missing directive. | Clickjacking exposure, unintended embedding by attacker-controlled origins, absent `frame-ancestors` on sensitive pages.[2][3] | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **navigate-to** | Restricts where a document can navigate, including certain form/JS-triggered navigations; it is part of CSP3 navigation control in the W3C spec and reflected in modern CSP documentation where supported.[7][3] | `*`, broad external destinations, missing directive. | Open redirect style navigation to untrusted domains, unrestricted top-level navigations from the page.[7][3] | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |

## Reporting directives

| Directive | Purpose | Insecure values to test for | Scanner should flag | Canonical W3C/MDN URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **report-uri** | Sends CSP violation reports to an endpoint; OWASP notes reporting directives are dependent on other directives and serve no protection by themselves.[2][3] | `http:` endpoints, broad third-party collectors, stale endpoints, missing TLS. | Plain-HTTP reporting endpoint, report exfiltration to untrusted collectors, obsolete-only reporting setup when `report-to` is available.[2][3] | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **report-to** | Uses the Reporting API as the preferred CSP violation reporting mechanism according to MDN.[3] | Untrusted reporting groups/endpoints, misconfigured group names, non-TLS collectors. | Reporting API endpoint abuse, misrouted reports, missing paired `Reporting-Endpoints` configuration.[3] | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |

## Other directives

| Directive | Purpose | Insecure values to test for | Scanner should flag | Canonical W3C/MDN URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **upgrade-insecure-requests** | Automatically upgrades HTTP subresource and navigation requests to HTTPS.[3] | Missing on HTTPS migrations, reliance on HTTP fallback paths. | HTTP resources still referenced in page content, mixed-content migration gaps, lack of upgrade enforcement on legacy content.[3] | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **block-all-mixed-content** | Blocks all mixed content on HTTPS pages.[7][3] | Missing directive on HTTPS pages that still reference HTTP subresources. | Mixed content present and no explicit block policy, passive/active mixed-content exposure.[7][3] | W3C: `https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/`[7]; MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **require-trusted-types-for** | Requires Trusted Types for specified sinks to mitigate DOM XSS.[3] | Absence on apps using dangerous DOM sinks; unsupported or missing enforcement. | Missing Trusted Types enforcement on DOM-XSS-prone apps, unsafe assignment sinks not protected by Trusted Types.[3] | MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |
| **trusted-types** | Declares allowed Trusted Types policy names and can help constrain which policies may create trusted values.[3] | `*`/overbroad policy allowance, missing restrictive policy naming, no Trusted Types policy governance. | Allow-all Trusted Types policy naming, missing governance around policy creation, weak migration posture for Trusted Types.[3] | MDN: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CSP`[3] |

## Weaknesses a security scanner should flag

A scanner should flag at least the following CSP weaknesses, because they are directly tied to the insecure values and deployment patterns documented in W3C/MDN/OWASP.[2][3][4][5]

- **Missing CSP entirely** or CSP only on the landing page instead of all responses.[3][2]
- **Wildcard source lists** such as `*` in any fetch directive, especially `script-src`, `default-src`, `connect-src`, `frame-src`, or `img-src`.[5][4]
- **`'unsafe-inline'`** in `script-src` or `style-src`, because it permits inline execution and is a major XSS enabler.[4][5]
- **`'unsafe-eval'`** in `script-src`, because it allows dynamic code evaluation such as `eval()`.[5][4]
- **`data:`** allowed for script, style, or other high-risk sinks where it is not strictly required.[4][5]
- **Overly broad third-party host allowlists** that are not strictly necessary for the app’s function.[3][5]
- **Missing `object-src 'none'`** in applications that should not load plugins/embedded objects.[4]
- **Missing `base-uri`**, which allows `<base>`-tag abuse to rewrite relative URL resolution.[2][3]
- **Missing `frame-ancestors`** on pages that must not be embedded, enabling clickjacking.[2][3]
- **Permissive `form-action`**, allowing exfiltration or phishing form posts.[2][3]
- **Permissive `navigate-to`**, allowing untrusted navigation targets.[7][3]
- **HTTP endpoints in `report-uri` or report collectors**, because violation reports can leak policy and page behavior to insecure endpoints.[2][3]
- **Failure to use `upgrade-insecure-requests` and/or `block-all-mixed-content`** on HTTPS sites that still reference HTTP resources.[3][7]
- **No Trusted Types enforcement** on applications with DOM XSS risk, or overly broad `trusted-types` policy allowance.[3]
- **Reliance on `child-src` alone** instead of modern `frame-src` and `worker-src` where precision is needed.[3]

## Notes on completeness

- The W3C CSP3 spec is the authoritative normative source for the directive framework.[7]
- MDN is the best browser-implementation reference among the provided sources and explicitly documents `require-trusted-types-for`, `trusted-types`, `upgrade-insecure-requests`, and `report-to`.[3]
- The provided sources do **not** mention a standardized CSP directive named **`webrtc`**; the GitHub issue explicitly asks for support links, which indicates it is *not* part of the documented baseline here.[9]

If you want, I can turn this into a **scanner rule matrix** with columns for *directive*, *positive test cases*, *negative test cases*, and *severity* for direct use in QA/security test plans.
